name: Scamander
description: Scamander, also known as Xanthus, was a river god in Greek mythology, embodying the Scamander River near Troy. He was the son of Oceanus and Tethys, making him one of the many river deities in the vast oceanic family. Scamander was a powerful god, his strength directly tied to the river's flow and the surrounding lands. He was known for his involvement in the Trojan War, siding with the Trojans against the Achaeans. During the war, Achilles, enraged by Hector's killing of Patroclus, pursued the Trojans relentlessly, eventually filling the Scamander River with corpses. Enraged, Scamander rose up in a furious torrent, attempting to drown Achilles. He was only subdued by Hephaestus, the god of fire, who was summoned by Hera to dry up the river with his flames. Scamander's power resided in his ability to control the river's currents, causing floods, creating whirlpools, and altering the landscape. He represented the raw, untamed power of nature and the consequences of disrespecting the natural world. He was venerated by the Trojans who relied on the river for sustenance and transportation. While ultimately defeated by Hephaestus, Scamander's role highlights the divine involvement in the Trojan War and the significance of rivers in the ancient Greek world. The river itself was considered sacred, and offerings were often made to appease the god and ensure the river's continued bounty.
subtitle: River God of Troy's Vital Waterway

keyMyths.title: Scamander's Wrath Against Achilles
keyMyths.description: During the Trojan War, Achilles polluted the Scamander River with corpses, angering the river god. Scamander unleashed a flood to drown Achilles, but Hephaestus intervened with fire.

keyMyths.title: Origin as Son of Oceanus and Tethys
keyMyths.description: Scamander is described as a son of Oceanus and Tethys, linking him to the primordial forces of the world's waters. This lineage establishes his status as a major river deity.

keyMyths.title: The River's Purifying Power
keyMyths.description: The Scamander River was sometimes seen as having purifying properties, capable of washing away sins and cleansing the body. This reinforces its spiritual importance.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Provides the main account of Scamander's role in the Trojan War.
